role_summary: Designer of the Black Panther Party logo at Stokely Carmichael's request (1966-67); SNCC Freedom Summer recruiter; co-editor of the Student Voice newsletter; later staff at the Center for Constitutional Rights.
biographical_paragraph: In 1966-67, when Stokely Carmichael asked for a graphic mark for the Black Panther Party, Dottie Zellner designed the logo that became the party's iconography [src: 2]. She had come to that work through SNCC, where she served as a recruiter for the 1964 Freedom Summer project and was co-editor of the organization's newsletter, the Student Voice [src: 2]. Born in 1938 in Manhattan, New York to Jewish immigrant parents from a secular, Yiddish-speaking, social-justice-oriented left, she graduated from Queens College, City University of New York [src: 2]. She married fellow SNCC activist Bob Zellner in 1962; the marriage later ended [src: 2]. Her later career took several turns: years working as a nurse, then in 1984 a move to the Center for Constitutional Rights, and in 1998 a directorship of publications and development at Queens College School of Law [src: 2]. Since 2002 she has been active in Israeli-Palestinian peace organizing and the Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions movement [src: 2]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 30 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
